flashcards.front: What is a viewing transformation in computer graphics?
flashcards.back: A mathematical process that converts 3D world coordinates to 2D screen coordinates, simulating how a camera captures visual information for display on output devices.

flashcards.front: Define View Reference Point (VRP) in the viewing coordinate system.
flashcards.back: The origin of the viewing coordinate system, typically positioned at the center of the viewing volume or at a point of interest in the scene.
A vector perpendicular to the view plane that defines the viewing direction. It determines which way the camera 'looks' at the scene.
A vector that specifies the upward direction on the view plane, used to orient the camera rotation around the viewing direction.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between perspective and parallel projection?
flashcards.back: Perspective projection: parallel lines converge to vanishing points, objects shrink with distance. Parallel projection: parallel lines remain parallel, no depth distortion.
A pyramidal volume that defines the region of 3D space visible to the camera. Bounded by front and back clipping planes and the view plane.
The process of mapping a rectangular region in world coordinates (window) to a rectangular region on the screen (viewport) for display.
The point from which projection rays emanate in perspective projection, or the direction of parallel projection rays.
Planes that bound the viewing frustum - front clipping plane (near) and back clipping plane (far) define what gets rendered.
Modeling transformation → World transformation → Viewing transformation → Projection → Clipping → Viewport transformation → Device coordinates.
A parallel projection where projection rays are perpendicular to the view plane, preserving parallel lines and relative proportions.

flashcards.front: What is a one-point perspective projection?
flashcards.back: A perspective projection where one set of parallel lines converges to a single vanishing point, typically when viewing an object head-on.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the normalization transformation in viewing?
flashcards.back: It transforms the viewing frustum into a canonical view volume (usually a unit cube) to simplify clipping and subsequent transformations.
A 2D coordinate system with origin typically at the upper-left corner of the screen, where x increases rightward and y increases downward.

flashcards.front: Define oblique projection in computer graphics.
flashcards.back: A parallel projection where projection rays are not perpendicular to the view plane, allowing visualization of multiple faces simultaneously.
